scenario: A DMN output column discount doesn't have a value list (no enumerated allowed values declared). The column is numeric. The team wonders if PRIORITY hit policy works on this column in Camunda 8.
question: Does PRIORITY hit policy work on output columns without a value list in Camunda 8?

options.text: PRIORITY hit policy works on any numeric output column and automatically sorts matching rules by their numeric output value to select the highest -- this describes behaviour from a hit policy that Camunda 8 does not support. 3 specification but is absent from Camunda 8's supported hit policy set. md are UNIQUE, ANY, FIRST, RULE ORDER, and COLLECT. PRIORITY has no runtime implementation in Zeebe.

PRIORITY hit policy selects the winning rule based on alphabetical ordering of output values when no explicit value list is defined -- this invents behaviour for a hit policy that Camunda 8 does not implement at all. Even setting aside the fabricated alphabetical behaviour, PRIORITY is not in the set of five hit policies supported by the Zeebe DMN engine. Attempting to use PRIORITY in a Camunda 8 decision table would result in a deployment validation error.

3 specification does define outputValues as an allowed-values constraint on output columns. More critically, PRIORITY hit policy is entirely absent from Camunda 8's supported hit policies regardless of whether output value lists are present, making the question's premise about PRIORITY semantics moot.

md:26-53 -- the supported set is exactly UNIQUE, ANY, FIRST, RULE ORDER, COLLECT (5 policies). 3 spec but Zeebe's DMN engine rejects it. For "single highest-priority output" semantics, the canonical C8 pattern is FIRST with rules ordered most-severe at the top -- author rules in priority order so the first matching rule's output is returned, short-circuiting the rest. For numeric "max value across rules", use COLLECT with a max() aggregator at the BPMN/FEEL layer (max(decisionResult)).

a.text: Incorrect. Per decision-table-hit-policy.md:26-53 verbatim ("The following hit policies are supported") C8 supports only 5 hit policies: UNIQUE, ANY, FIRST, RULE ORDER, COLLECT. PRIORITY and OUTPUT ORDER are explicitly absent even though they exist in DMN-1.3. Pattern #7 (10th cumulative hit across audit) -- exam questions framing PRIORITY as a real C8 policy are testing whether you know the C8 supported-set, not generic DMN-1.3 knowledge. Canonical C8 replacements for "single highest-priority output": FIRST with rules in priority order: author rules so the most-severe / highest-priority condition is at the top. FIRST short-circuits at the first match per :91-99. Adding a catch-all default at the bottom (all inputs -) ensures a result. COLLECT + downstream max(): when "highest numeric value" across multiple matching rules is needed, set hitPolicy="COLLECT" and aggregate with max(decisionResult) (or min()) at the calling FEEL/BPMN layer. COLLECT with built-in aggregator: DMN-1.3 spec allows COLLECT with MIN/MAX/SUM/COUNT aggregators in the aggregation attribute on . C8's coverage of COLLECT aggregators varies by version -- verify if you need engine-level aggregation vs FEEL-side aggregation. About output value lists: the DMN-1.3 spec defines outputValues (allowed-values lists on output columns). Camunda 8 documentation does NOT describe a runtime enforcement mechanism for output value lists (Pattern #42 -- 0 grep hits for outputValues|allowed values in docs/components/modeler/dmn/). Type checking is via typeRef against the 9 DMN-data-types per dmn-data-types.md. Enum-style enforcement requires rule coverage discipline + upstream FEEL guards.

b.text: Incorrect. PRIORITY isn't in C8's supported set; the "numeric sort" framing is a fabrication.

c.text: Incorrect. No alphabetical default in any C8 policy; PRIORITY isn't supported regardless.

d.text: Correct. Output value lists exist in the DMN-1.3 spec but C8 doesn't document enforcement; the question's PRIORITY premise is moot.

explanation: Output value lists exist in the DMN-1.3 spec but C8 doesn't document enforcement; the question's PRIORITY premise is moot.
